name: Copper Gulch
short_description: High-elevation BLM site in Beaver County featuring copper and malachite specimens in a moderate-slope canyon setting. Free public rockhounding with a 25 lb daily collection limit.
description:
Copper Gulch sits at 7,106 feet elevation on BLM-managed public land in Beaver County, Utah, offering rockhounders access to copper-bearing deposits formed within the region's extensive mining district. The site's moderate slope and canyon environment create ideal conditions for surface collecting of oxidized copper minerals, particularly malachite and chrysocolla, which weather out from the underlying copper-rich host rock.

This area benefits from Utah's rich mining heritage. The Beaver County region has a long history of copper and precious metal exploration, and Copper Gulch itself sits within geologically favorable terrain where Paleozoic sedimentary rocks and Tertiary-age intrusions created the mineral concentrations found here today. The oxidized zone (the weathered upper part of ore bodies) is where collectors find the most accessible and colorful specimens.

What to expect: The terrain is moderately steep with good access from BLM roads. Collectors typically find malachite in various forms - from bright green crusts on rock surfaces to solid nodules in washes and talus slopes. Chrysocolla (a blue-green copper silicate) is also present and highly prized for its color. The elevation means cooler conditions year-round and seasonal snow in winter, which can affect access.

The site is best worked by hiking into the gulch from parking areas and following the drainage where water has exposed fresh material. The moderate slope means you will encounter uneven footing and loose rock, typical of high-altitude canyon terrain. Unlike fee dig sites, there is no guarantee of finds - success depends on geological luck and thorough searching, which is part of the appeal for experienced rockhounders.

Copper Gulch rewards collectors who understand copper oxidation chemistry and know what to look for: bright green (malachite), pale blue (chrysocolla), and deeper blue (azurite) minerals on or near copper-stained host rock. The combination of public access, free collecting, and genuine collectible material makes this a legitimate destination for Utah rockhounders.
history: This area sits within the Beaver County mining district, where Tertiary-age porphyry copper deposits formed as magmatic fluids deposited copper sulfides in fractured Paleozoic host rocks. The oxidation of these primary sulfide minerals over millions of years created the malachite, chrysocolla, and azurite that collectors find today - a natural concentration process that makes this gulch attractive for surface rockhounding.
tips: Search the wash bottom and along the canyon walls where water erosion has freshly exposed copper-stained material - malachite and chrysocolla often appear as bright green or pale blue mineral coatings or small nodules on weathered rock. Use a 10x hand lens to identify fine-grained chrysocolla and azurite, which can be easily mistaken for stained rock until magnified. Bring a small sledge hammer and chisel to carefully extract specimens from host rock, but focus first on loose pieces in talus - they are often better quality and easier to collect. Work higher on the slope after rain when fresh material is exposed, but avoid the gulch bottom during flash flood season (July - September) when afternoon storms can cause rapid water flow.
terrain_notes: Located at 7,106 feet elevation with moderate slope throughout the gulch. The terrain consists of exposed bedrock, talus fields, and a central wash drainage. Vegetation is sparse high-altitude scrub and scattered piñon-juniper. Walking distance from parking is typically 0.5 to 2 miles depending on which pullout you use. Footing is uneven and rocky - sturdy hiking boots are essential. The slope makes this moderately strenuous, especially at elevation, but is not technical or dangerous for careful hikers.
best_season: May through October. The high elevation (7,106 ft) means the site receives snow and is less accessible November through April. Best collecting is June through September when snow is gone and trails are fully open, though afternoon thunderstorms are common July through August. Fall (September - October) offers excellent weather with lower storm risk.
written_directions: From Beaver, Utah (about 30 miles south), take US Highway 89 south toward Panguitch. Continue approximately 15 miles south of Beaver. Look for BLM access roads heading east toward the canyon drainages in Beaver County. Copper Gulch can be accessed via several unmarked BLM pullouts along the foothills. Use a detailed Utah county map or GPS to locate the specific gulch between Highway 89 and the mountain ridge. High-clearance vehicle recommended for final approach roads, though passenger vehicles can reach trailheads in dry conditions. Parking areas consist of informal pullouts off rough dirt roads.
road_conditions: Rough dirt BLM roads with washboard surface. Some ruts and rocks. High-clearance vehicle recommended but passenger cars can reach main trailheads in good weather. Not suitable for low-clearance or towed vehicles.
parking_notes: Informal BLM pullouts and turnarounds along access roads. No developed parking lot. Space for 2-4 vehicles at typical pullout locations. No facilities.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA (Federal Land Policy and Management Act). No commercial collection. No motorized excavation or power tools. Hand tools only (hammer, chisel, pick). Pack out all trash. Respect any posted closure signs.
rules:

nearest_services: Beaver, Utah, approximately 30 miles north. Gas, food, lodging, and supplies available. No services at or near the site.
accessibility_notes: Moderate difficulty terrain with significant hiking required over uneven rocky slopes. No wheelchair access. Sturdy hiking boots and reasonable fitness required. Elevation (7,106 ft) may cause fatigue for visitors from lower elevations. Trail is unmarked and requires navigation skill or GPS. Not suitable for limited mobility or young children under 8.

faq.question: What copper minerals can you find at Copper Gulch near Beaver, Utah?
faq.answer: Copper Gulch is known for malachite (bright green), chrysocolla (pale blue-green), and azurite (deep blue). These secondary copper minerals form from the weathering of primary copper deposits. Malachite is the most common and easiest to identify, often appearing as coatings on weathered rocks or small nodules in talus. Chrysocolla requires closer inspection with a 10x hand lens but is highly valued for its color and rarity.

faq.question: Is rockhounding free at Copper Gulch, and do you need a permit?
faq.answer: Yes, rockhounding at Copper Gulch is free and requires no permit. It is located on BLM public land managed under recreational use guidelines. You can collect up to 25 lbs per day for personal use at no cost. No commercial collection is allowed, and no advance permit application is needed.

faq.question: How far is Copper Gulch from Beaver, Utah, and what is the drive like?
faq.answer: Copper Gulch is approximately 30 miles south of Beaver near the Beaver County foothills. The drive takes about 45 minutes via US Highway 89, followed by rough BLM access roads that require high-clearance vehicles or passengers cars in good weather. The final approach involves unpaved dirt roads with washboard surface and occasional ruts. Best to bring a detailed Utah county map or GPS coordinates.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound at Copper Gulch?
faq.answer: May through October is the best season, with June through September offering the longest access window. The site sits at 7,106 feet elevation and receives snow November through April, making winter access difficult or impossible. September and October provide excellent weather with lower afternoon thunderstorm risk compared to July and August. Avoid the site during and after heavy rains when flash floods are a risk in canyons.

faq.question: What tools and skills do you need to collect at Copper Gulch?
faq.answer: Bring a small sledge hammer, chisel, and pick for extracting specimens from host rock. A 10x hand lens is essential for identifying fine-grained chrysocolla and azurite. Sturdy hiking boots are required for the uneven rocky terrain at moderate slope. A detailed map or GPS is needed since trails are unmarked. No motorized tools are allowed. Beginners can find loose specimens in talus and washes without needing specialized skills.

faq.question: Is Copper Gulch a good rockhounding site for beginners and families?
faq.answer: Copper Gulch is beginner-friendly in terms of access and collection rules, but the moderate difficulty terrain and high elevation require reasonable fitness and careful navigation. Children should be at least 8 years old. The site is free and open to the public, with no permit needed. Unlike fee dig sites, success depends on geological luck and thorough searching. The colorful copper minerals are easy to identify once found, making this an educational experience for families interested in natural geology and mineral collecting.

meta_description: Find malachite, chrysocolla, and azurite at Copper Gulch, a free BLM rockhounding site near Beaver, Utah. No permit required. 25 lb daily limit.
